body:
### Problem

Problem:
Since https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38354 there is now logging on semantic tokens errors. However due to that the `lsp.log` file gets filled with `Content modified` errors when using `lua-language-server` (probably other lsp's too). This happens due to fast text changes and those aren't actually errors.

Solution:
Either remove the `vim.lsp.log.error("semantic_tokens", err)` lines in `runtime/lua/vim/lsp/semantic_tokens.lua` or change them to `log.debug` or `log.info` or something else.

### Steps to reproduce using "nvim --clean -u minimal_init.lua"

minimal_init.lua:
```
vim.lsp.config("lua_ls", {
  cmd = { "lua-language-server" },
  filetypes = { "lua" },
})
vim.lsp.enable("lua_ls")
```

1. `nvim --clean -u minimal_init.lua minimal_init.lua` (or any lua file)
2. Start typing and changing text on multiple lines
3. Open the log file that is pointed to in `:checkhealth vim.lsp`
4. See the `"semantic_tokens"` errors due to `"Content modified."`

### Expected behavior

I expect the `lsp.log` file to not be filled with semantic tokens non-errors.

### Nvim version (nvim -v)

0.12.2

### Language server name/version

lua-language-server 3.18.1

### Operating system/version

NixOS 26.05

### Log file

_No response_

comment_data.body: This seems to be an error that was previously (undeliberately) ignored and has now been recorded. I guess issue #40224 resolved this problem. I don’t have time to verify it for now. CC @jdrouhard

> I guess issue [#40224](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40224) resolved this problem.

@ivangeorgiew can you try latest nightly

It doesn't resolve these "Content modified" errors (I just checked).

I turned up my logging to `DEBUG` and triggered this a few times. It happens specifically when a semantic tokens request is in flight (server hasn't responded yet), and we send another `textDocument/didChange`. lua-language-server errors out the in-flight one with that message.

I'm not sure there's a lot we can do here...

> I'm not sure there's a lot we can do here...

I think we can dedupe messages. That's a general pattern that all of our logging everywhere should handle well.

> It happens specifically when a semantic tokens request is in flight (server hasn't responded yet), and we send another textDocument/didChange.

Does this mean we should send a `$/cancel` request in situations like this? It's possible that the issue isn't limited to semantic tokens; it's just that they are requested the most frequently.

I noticed #40224 previously. If we use `buf_attach` instead of `LspNotify`, it might result in us sending a semantic token/code lens request before we even notify the LSP of the changes. Therefore, I originally planned to use `LspNotify`, or to handle this within `Capability`. We might need to do more detailed tracking？ Just leave as a reminder.

> Does this mean we should send a `$/cancel` request in situations like this? It's possible that the issue isn't limited to semantic tokens; it's just that they are requested the most frequently.

Possibly, but the whole point of document synchronization and document versioning is to make this kind of stuff "just work" so the `$/cancel` seems like extra effort for something that shouldn't be required.

> I noticed #40224 previously. If we use `buf_attach` instead of `LspNotify`, it might result in us sending a semantic token/code lens request before we even notify the LSP of the changes. Therefore, I originally planned to use `LspNotify`, or to handle this within `Capability`. We might need to do more detailed tracking？ Just leave as a reminder.

`LspNotify' is definitely the better solution than any custom debounce/wait solution in each module. It just didn't exist yet when I first wrote semantic token support. Moving it into Capability might work too if we added callbacks (like on_attach) when document close, open, and changed events are fired.

As for more detailed tracking--we do already know everything in progress, so we _could_ trigger cancels for pending requests when we know the document version changes, but this isn't as easy as it sounds. I use something like this in my own config to track requests that may end up "jumping" somewhere else and cancel them all on cursor moved events. However, there are a lot of request types you probably don't want to cancel if the document version changes.

> Possibly, but the whole point of document synchronization and document versioning is to make this kind of stuff "just work" so the $/cancel seems like extra effort for something that shouldn't be required.

Agree that it shouldn't be
required. However this be a worthwhile optimization. Previously, `vim.diagnostic` significantly improved performance by explicitly sending a `$/cancel` request, preventing the Lua language server from pointlessly diagnosing code in outdated buffers.

The Capability-based idea I was referring to is to have it called directly at the point where the document synchronization logic occurs (i.e., where `LspNotify` is triggered). This approach would require significant code changes, so I am not sure if it is worth it. So simply using `LspNotify` seems to provide a lot of leverage at this moment.

-32801 is an explicitly defined error code in the LSP. I checked the spec, and it states:

> The server detected that the content of a document got modified outside normal conditions. A server should NOT send this error code if it detects a content change in its unprocessed messages. The result even computed on an older state might still be useful for the client.
> 
> If a client decides that a result is not of any use anymore the client should cancel the request.

I think the spec makes it very clear. If we strictly follow the specification, my understanding is:

- This is indeed a bug in lua ls. Even if `didChange` is sent, it should still return the result for the older version of the buffer instead of an error message.
- For nvim, we indeed do not need the semantic tokens result for the older version of the buffer, so we should send `$/cancel`.
- This is a common pattern, so handling the request within the document synchronization logic does make sense.

In practice, however, the mutually imperfect implementation of lua ls and nvim only results in this error being logged, without any other impact.

> - For nvim, we indeed do not need the semantic tokens result for the older version of the buffer, so we should send `$/cancel`

We already do this. 

> - This is a common pattern, so handling the request within the document synchronization logic does make sense.
> 
> In practice, however, the mutually imperfect implementation of lua ls and nvim only results in this error being logged, without any other impact.


I think that means we can file a bug with LuaLS but otherwise just accept the additional logging noise until it's fixed there?

labels.description: wrong behavior that was introduced in a previous commit (please bisect)
labels.name: bug-regression

title: LSP: `lsp.log` gets filled with `"semantic_tokens"	{ code = -32801, message = "Content modified."` on fast text change
